title: Independent regulators and financial stability evidence from gubernatorial election campaigns in the Progressive Era
abstract: Regulatory independence forms a foundation for modern financial systems. The institutions’ value is illuminated by a Progressive Era policy experiment when independent state-bank regulators came under governors’ supervision. Afterwards, bank resolution rates declined during gubernatorial election campaigns for banks supervised by state but not national authorities. This gubernatorial-campaign effect diminished by two orders of magnitude, but did not disappear, after the FDIC became the independent resolver for all insured banks in 1935. In addition, during the Progressive Era, declines in bank resolutions led to declines in business bankruptcy rates, an effect that is not observed in the FDIC era. Our findings indicate regulatory independence can dramatically reduce but may not eliminate politics’ impact on banks and the economy.

author.Marco Del Angel*: California State University, Los Angeles, United States
author.Gary Richardson: Tsinghua University, China, National Bureau of Economic Research, University of California, Irvine, United States

article.1. Introduction: A recent Supreme Court decision, Seila Law v. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), ruled the CFPB's independence from Presidential oversight was unconstitutional (Supreme Court, United States 2020).1 This case is a spearpoint for a series of suits seeking to overturn Progressive Era precedents underlying independence of federal agencies.2 The campaign could compel federal agencies to operate under political supervision, where their executives serve at the will of the President, rather than independently, where their executives can be removed only for good cause.3 This possibility raises questions concerning optimal structures for government agencies, particularly those regulating financial institutions, for which independence may be particularly important. Is independence beneficial? Would political subservience alter regulators’ decisions regarding financial institutions such as closing failing banks, a process called bank resolution?A Progressive Era policy experiment can serve as a laboratory for these issues. Lessons drawn from that period are useful for policymakers in the present, particularly those regarding monetary institutions and financial regulation, which evolve over time slowly. Between 1903 and 1922, legislatures in half of all states shifted decisions regarding when and how to resolve troubled banks from judges to bureaucrats. Judges operated independent of political oversight. Bureaucrats worked for governors who could remove them at will and allocate the positions for political patronage (Komai and Richardson, 2014; Mitchener and Jaremski, 2015). This shift occurred in different states at different times. It did not affect supervision of banks with national charters; decisions about their resolutions remained with the Comptroller of Currency. It did not affect businesses other than commercial banks; decisions about their bankruptcies remained with federal bankruptcy courts or, in the case of railroads, with federal regulators. We ask how subordinating the bank resolution process to political supervision influenced bank-resolution rates, particularly during gubernatorial election campaigns, when governors had strong incentives to delay resolutions in hopes that better news about the economy would yield votes at the ballot box, and whether reductions in resolutions due to gubernatorial campaigns, in turn, impacted business bankruptcy rates.To analyze the impact of this policy innovation, we create new data series of bank resolution and business bankruptcy rates by state and quarter from 1903 through 1929. We focus on this period because it spans the policy experiment and due to the nature of the data. Our sources report consistent information on business bankruptcies and bank resolutions beginning in 1903 following the Bankruptcy Act of 1898 and ending with legal changes during the early 1930s. Our analysis employs an instrument whose effect has been observed in recent decades, gubernatorial election campaigns (Liu and Ngo, 2014).We find that after governors began supervising resolutions of troubled banks, resolutions declined substantially during gubernatorial campaigns. Regulators reduced the resolution rate to zero in one-third of all gubernatorial campaigns when they had the opportunity to do so and in many additional campaigns deferred some but not all resolutions. The magnitude of this gubernatorial-campaign effect during the Progressive Era (1903–1929) was more than 100 times the magnitude measured in recent decades (1976–2012). The magnitude was even larger during times of financial distress, like the Panics of 1907 and 1914 and the agricultural lending crisis of the 1920s. Our difference-in-difference methods demonstrate that the change in resolution regime – from politically independence to politically subservience – led to the reduction in resolutions during gubernatorial campaigns. These reductions only occurred for state-chartered banks and only in states where bureaucrats subordinate to the governor controlled the resolution process. These reductions did not occur for nationally chartered banks (which were not supervised by state authorities) or during campaigns for federal offices (federal officials did not supervise state-chartered banks). It did not occur in states where judges, rather than regulators under the governor's jurisdiction, supervised bank resolutions.Our results indicate gubernatorial campaigns in states whose governors supervised bank resolutions can serve as an instrument in a two-stage procedure where we first estimate how gubernatorial campaigns influenced bank resolutions and then use that result to estimate how bank resolutions influenced business bankruptcies. It is important to note that the exclusion criterion holds. Progressive Era governors and their subordinates lacked the ability to directly defer business bankruptcies, since creditors determined when to file suit and adjudication occurred in federal courts, and had little ability to indirectly influence these rates, since governors lacked the economic information and policy tools that allow them to intervene in the economy today. A range of estimation methods yield similar, substantial results. During political campaigns when governors’ subordinates deferred all bank resolutions until after the election, business bankruptcy rates declined from 9% to 33% of the sample mean. A 33% decline was equivalent to the reduction in business failure rates between the winter (Q1), when roughly 2.8 businesses failed per thousand, to the summer (Q3), when 2.1 businesses failed per thousand. It is also similar to the decline in bankruptcies during an economic boom like that which occurred during World War 1 or the Roaring 20s.Our paper contributes to several literatures in the social sciences. The first examines the impact of gubernatorial campaigns on financial and economic activity. Recent research shows uncertainty preceding gubernatorial elections reduces the number and prices of initial public offerings as well as investment by firms (Çolak, Durnev, and Qian, 2017; Jens, 2017). The closest paper to ours and an inspiration for our work is Liu and Ngo (2014). This seminal essay demonstrates that between 1976 and 2010, resolutions of FDIC insured banks declined preceding gubernatorial elections, except during the years of the S&L Crisis and Global Financial Crisis, when gubernatorial campaigns appear to have had no or an inverse impact on resolution rates. While their results were “consistent with the view that political control tends to lead to more corrupt behavior. The mechanism through which this occurs is less clear,” because the FDIC is an independent federal agency not subject to gubernatorial oversight (Liu and Ngo 2014, p. 267). We examine the pre-FDIC era, before the creation of a politically independent agency specialized in resolving troubled banks and when the mechanism was clear. Regulators subordinate to governors delayed resolving banks during gubernatorial campaigns. Under this mechanism from 1903 to 1929, election campaigns’ impact on bank resolution rates was 140 to 169 times larger than under the FDIC from 1976 to 2012. In addition, reductions in resolutions during gubernatorial campaigns in the Progressive Era peaked during financial crises and impacted firms that borrowed from banks, unlike in modern times. Establishing the FDIC as a politically independent resolver of troubled banks, in other words, dramatically reduced political influence on resolution decisions during times of crisis when most financial institutions failed and when bank failures impacted broader economic activity.The second literature investigates independence of financial regulators, which is a core principle underlying bank supervision in OECD countries. Regulatory independence increases financial stability, limits political uncertainty, reduces corruption, solves time-consistency problems, and shapes perceptions of investors and flows of capital (Bertelli and Whitford, 2009; Dincer and Eichengreen, 2012; Lockhart 2017; Barth et al., 2004). Independence of bank regulators also influences decisions and independence of central banks (Kohn 2013; Hüpkes et al., 2006). Most empirical work on the issue is international. We provide evidence that regulatory independence influenced bank stability and economic performance in the United States. We also show that incentives of state regulators a century ago led them to be more lenient than their federal counterparts, as Agarwal et al. (2014) found recently. Subordinating financial regulators to politicians aligned their behavior closer to politicians’ priorities and farther from the publics, as Besley and Coate (2003) argue is the case for modern industries.The third is the political economy of American elections. A large literature finds that voters hold incumbent politicians accountable for economic conditions and that governors strive to send signals that they are working hard to improve the economy (Brown, 2010; Slattery and Zidar, 2020). Historians trace governors’ efforts to generate good economic news back to the Great Depression and subsequent expansion of government (Turner, 2003). We show that governors’ efforts began at least thirty years earlier during the Progressive Era.The fourth is on the role of banks in the economy and the relationship between bank distress and economic activity. Some scholars argue that bank failures have little impact on the economy because bank credit has many substitutes. Other scholars argue that bank failures reduce production and commerce because banks solve information problems that limit credit available to small or specialized firms (Bernanke, 1983; Mitchener and Richardson, 2019; Richardson and Troost, 2009; Ramirez and Shively, 2012). We are the first scholars to use an exogenous instrument (gubernatorial elections) and a policy experiment (introduction of political control of the bank resolution process) to demonstrate a causal link between bank resolutions and business activity during the Progressive Era.A fifth literature examines how political interference in finance impact developing countries. One strand of this literature studies political business cycles in emerging markets (Brown and Dinc, 2005; Carvalho, 2014; Cole, 2009; Dinc, 2005). Another line of research studies effects of political connections on economic outcomes. In this literature, a principal question concerns whether politically connected firms receive preferential access to finance, how this influenced their profitability and growth, and whether it affects the structure and efficiency of industries and markets (Claessens et al., 2008; Khwaja and Mian, 2005; Li et al., 2008). We show that a century ago, when the U.S. was an emerging market economy and government began regulating financial and industrial firms, politicians often intervened in ways in which influenced economic outcomes to the advantage of politically connected banks and borrowers as well as the advantage of those in power. The adoption of independent regulators, like the FDIC, limited but did not eliminate this problem.The rest of our essay corroborates our claims. Section 2 describes the policy experiment, the nature of the data, and qualitative evidence that governors and their subordinates manipulated the bank-resolution process during the Progressive Era. Section 3 provides a first look at the data and compares the magnitude of the gubernatorial-campaign effect during the Progressive Era to the rest of the twentieth century. Section 4 statistically analyzes the impact of gubernatorial campaigns on bank resolution rates from 1903 to 1929. Section 5 presents two-stage estimates indicating how gubernatorial campaigns’ impact on banks in turn impacted business firms. A series of appendices describe the construction of the data, discuss details of our calculations, verify the robustness our results, and explore alternative estimators.Section 6 discusses the implications of our analysis. Our findings are directly relevant to the current policy debate, which is what would happen if the Supreme Court eliminated political independence of federal financial regulators. Liu and Ngo's (2014) pioneering paper showed that the FDIC's political insulation is imperfect today. Our essay shows that if you eliminate the FDIC's political independence entirely, the situation could be much worse.
article.2. Historical background and qualitative: This section discusses facts important for understanding our analysis. The discussion begins with resolutions of banks. Their nature differed across states and over time. In some states, creditors and courts determined when banks failed. In other states, regulators subordinate to governors determined when banks failed. In the later but not the former, newspapers reported many cases in which regulators delayed bank resolutions during gubernatorial elections at the behest (or the explicit direction) of the incumbent. Our discussion concludes with bankruptcies of businesses. Their timing was determined by decisions of debtors, creditors, and judges under national laws adjudicated in federal courts. This process differed markedly from methods of resolving troubled banks.2.1. Bank Resolutions and the Dual Banking SystemDuring the Progressive Era, commercial banks operated within the borders of a single state, and almost always, within a single city. In every state, about two-thirds of all banks possessed state charters, while about one-third of all banks operated under national charters. The process for resolving banks that could not pay their debts differed between national and state-chartered banks. The Office of the Comptroller of Currency (OCC) regulated national banks and determined how to resolve troubled institutions (High 1910, p. 442; Komai and Richardson, 2014). The OCC was an independent agency in the U.S. Department of Treasury whose director, the Comptroller, was appointed by the President and confirmed by the Senate. Comptrollers were appointed for five-year terms, received high salaries, and could only be removed from office with the concurrence of the President and Senate. The length of appointment and limits on removal were unique among federal appointees. Congress structured the office in this manner to limit partisan influence on bank supervision and resolution.The OCC did not supervise state-chartered banks. Before 1900, “many states had no supervision of banks and trust companies (Board of Governors 1932, p. 11).” Instead, regulation of state-chartered banks was left to depositors and courts. Depositors shifted funds from banks they deemed too risky and sued banks that failed to make payment as promised. After ascertaining the existence of unpaid debts, judges would take possession of a bank and appoint a receiver to liquidate the corporation and repay claimants.About half of all states established bank regulatory agencies in the nineteenth century. The rest established them between 1900 and the mid-1920s (Mitchener and Jaremski 2015, pp. 853–4). These agencies regulated and oversaw resolutions of state but not national banks, particularly after the Bankruptcy Act of 1898 excluded suits about banks (both state and national) from federal bankruptcy courts. Bank superintendents and their staffs routinely examined banks, determined which banks were insolvent or might in the future fail to fulfill their obligations, and decided whether those banks could be saved by remedial action or should be shut down to protect depositors’ rights and the public's interests. Superintendents also determined whether banks that suspended payments to depositors should be liquidated or allowed time to fix their finances. Regulators had a great deal of discretion. The law required regulators to provide banks with opportunities to correct problems if possible before taking stronger action. Regulators were compelled to act aggressively only when depositors faced imminent losses and delay threated to exacerbate harms.While laws creating bank supervisory agencies gave regulators wide discretion, these laws also limited depositors and the judiciary's role in the process. The laws typically forbade depositors from “litigation which would likely result in [a bank's] premature dissolution (Tardy and Smith, 1920, p. 1231).” Instead,laws regarding banking institutions generally provide a system of legal or administrative procedure under which an institution which is insolvent or is being improperly conducted may be given an opportunity to rehabilitate itself or remove the cause for criticism in its management before being subjected to liquidation (Tardy and Smith, 1920, p. 1231).Creditors, in other words, could not force a bank into liquidation by suing in court. Only regulators had the authority to initiate resolutions.Fluctuations in resolution rates reflected regulatory decisions within states as well as ebbs and flows of economic activity (Richardson, 2007). Quarterly resolution rates for state-chartered banks in the 48 contiguous states (excluding Wyoming) from 1903 to 1929 exhibited striking patterns, which are displayed in Table 1. The average was 2.42 resolutions per thousand banks. The median, however, was zero. No banks failed, in other words, in most quarters in most states. Resolution rates varied over time and the business cycle. In years without financial crises from 1903 to 1919, resolution rates were low, with an average of 1 resolution per quarter per 1000 banks. Rates in these years were similar to those for commercial banks in typical years from 1934 to 2012. Liu and Ngo's (2014) analysis observed the gubernatorial campaign effect in typical years but not during times of crisis, particularly the surge in resolutions due to the S&L Crisis (1986–92) and Global Financial Crisis (2007–2010).Table 1. Both stemmed from agricultural expansions fueled by low-cost government loans and high prices on world markets. Both began when world crop prices declined, and farmers’ debts exceeded their ability to repay. During both, banks struggled in agricultural states in the South, Midwest, and Great Plains. The crisis of the 1920s raised resolution rates to 4.8 per quarter per thousand banks.Financial crises akin to those of the Great Recession occurred periodically in the Progressive Era. These events included the Panic of 1907, whose economic and financial consequences lasted through 1908; the Panic of 1914, which began with the world wars’ onset in August and resolved in a few months; and the Depression of 1920–1. That contraction's amplitude, length, and impact on financial institutions resembled the contraction from 2007 to 2009. During these years (1907–8, 1914, 1920–1), quarterly resolution rates rose to 2.26 per thousand banks.2.2. Political influence and bank resolutionsThe resolution process, which gave regulators discretion over when banks closed, provided regulators and the politicians whom they served with opportunities to use that discretion to satisfy a variety of objectives. Governors had good reasons for encouraging regulators to defer resolutions during gubernatorial campaigns, which lasted six to nine months at the turn of the twentieth century (Kleppner 1981 and 1987). Incumbents believed that they received more votes during economic booms and fewer votes during busts. Bank resolutions served as signals of economic distress and were widely noted. Almost all bank failures – no matter how small – were reported in national newspapers of record, like the New York Times and Wall Street Journal, as well as local newspapers. Bank resolutions often triggered legal investigations leading to civil suits against stockholders and indictments and prosecutions of senior executives. These suits generated continuing streams of news accounts (Koch et al., 2020). Governors and the bank superintendents that worked for them were often blamed for bank failures. Partisan political accusations concerning insufficient, misguided, or malfeasant financial supervision were prominently covered in the popular press. In New York, this issue recurred regularly, with the governor and his bank superintendent being accused of laxity, incompetence, or political favoritism in the years 1906, 1911, 1920, 1923, and 1929.4 Other governors in New York touted effective supervision of banks and passage of stronger banking laws as accomplishments worthy of reelection.5Similar news coverage appeared in many other states including Alaska, Georgia, Illinois, Kansas, Massachusetts, Michigan, North Carolina, Ohio, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, and Tennessee.6 Notorious examples come from Oklahoma. The states’ first and fourth governors were accused of directing state officials to delay resolution of banks that ultimately failed with large losses to depositors. Newspapers nationwide covered these accusations – including allegations of bribery, extortion, double-crossing, and murder – because they were salacious, public, partisan, and prominent during political campaigns.7These newspaper accounts tell a consistent tale. Governors appointed leaders of agencies that regulated banks. These appointees had discretion regarding how to handle troubled banks. The law encouraged regulators to extend time to banks trying to correct deficiencies. During gubernatorial campaigns, regulators often provided more time than usual. These delays limited bad economic news in the run up to the election, improving the incumbent's chances.We document these stories by citing scores of newspaper articles on the topic which we found by searching the Proquest newspaper database. Our searches revealed hundreds of articles about governors and their subordinates influencing the resolution of state-chartered banks. We found close to zero stories about politicians attempting to influence outcomes for nationally chartered banks and no stories about politicians successfully doing so. This state-versus-federal difference has been noted by researchers past and present. The Federal Reserve's Committee on Branch, Group, and Chain banking wrote “there was also a greater possibility of direct political influence under state than under national charters (Board of Governors 1932, p. 11).” In contrast, the OCC had more political independence, tougher regulations, and a tradition of strict enforcement (Board of Governors, 1932).Fig. 1 provides initial graphical evidence that resolutions of state-chartered banks declined during gubernatorial campaigns, which typically spanned the two quarters preceding gubernatorial elections. Panel (A), which displays counts of all state bank resolutions preceding and following elections, indicates that hundreds of fewer banks failed during gubernatorial campaigns than at other times. Panel (B), which displays the fraction of observations with one or more resolutions, indicates the fraction of quarters with resolutions was about 10 percentage points lower during gubernatorial campaigns than in quarters following elections. Years of financial crises include 1907–8 (Panic of 1907), 1914 (Panic of 1914), and 1920–21 (Post WW1 Depression and Deflation). Years containing agricultural lending crises include 1922 to 1929.2.3. Businesses Bankruptcy and the Bankruptcy Act of 1898The Bankruptcy Act of 1898 standardized procedures for bankruptcies throughout the United States (Black, 1898; Warren,1935; Skeel, 2001; Hansen, 2001). All bankruptcies of businesses, except railroads and banks, were adjudicated in federal courts. Timing was determined by creditors, who could choose to file suit at any time after debtors failed to make required payments. Judges who supervised the process held lifetime tenures. They could be removed from office only after investigation and impeachment. Individual judges dealt with small proportions of cases and lacked ability to influence aggregate failure rates. State officials – including governors, legislators, and judges – had no authority over the timing of bankruptcy filings or the adjudication of cases. Governors and their subordinates, in other words, lacked the ability to directly influence business bankruptcy rates by intervening in the judicial process.Governors also lacked the ability to indirectly influence business bankruptcy rates in a substantive way for several reasons. First, states typically passed balanced budgets, and state government budgets were small. Expenditures of all 48 states summed to less than one percent of gross domestic product (GDP), which is much smaller than the 14% of GDP that state governments expend today (Stiglitz, 2015).8 Second, laisse faire was the prevailing ethos. Progressive Era lawmakers increasingly regulated business activity to protect consumers and the public, curtail monopolies, and limit abuses by big businesses, but neither subsidized nor bailed-out substantial numbers of firms (or entire industries) until the New Deal during the Great Depression. Third, state legislatures controlled spending, not governors. Legislators and governors did not have symmetric incentives, and we find no evidence that bank resolutions declined during campaigns for state legislatures (except for those coinciding with gubernatorial campaigns). Fourth, governments then like governments today found it difficult to use fiscal policy to influence economic activity rapidly in short spans of time. Governments had little timely statistical data on fluctuations in the state economy. Fiscal policy worked with long and variable lags. Fiscal policy's impact was debatable and unpredictable. So, state legislatures seldom intervened to counteract business fluctuations in general and to our knowledge never crafted fiscal policies that expanded spending during gubernatorial campaigns and contracted spending immediately after gubernatorial elections.While governors lacked authority to directly determine the timing of business bankruptcies and had little or no ability to indirectly influence this timing, business bankruptcies did decline during gubernatorial campaigns, as Fig. 3 shows. Given the small size and limited abilities of state governments in the Progressive Era, it seems unlikely that governors systematically took actions other than delaying resolutions of banks that lowered business bankruptcy rates during political campaigns. We know of no evidence indicating that governors seeking reelection directly bailed out substantial numbers of bankrupt businesses or implemented fiscal policies that rapidly rejuvenated the local economy. Later in this essay, we will point to three types of statistical evidence that indicates governors did not, in fact, do this. Section 4 will show that resolution rates of nationally chartered banks remained constant during gubernatorial campaigns. If governors had taken actions that boosted the state economy or alleviated distress for substantial numbers of business firms, all commercial banks should have benefited, not just those with state charters. Section 5 will show that business bankruptcy rates did not decline during gubernatorial campaigns in states where judges controlled the bank resolution process. These rates should have fallen, however, if governors used fiscal policy or other mechanisms to systematically lower business failure rates during gubernatorial campaigns. Section 5 will also show that business bankruptcies did not decline during gubernatorial campaigns in recent decades, an era when state governments were much larger than in the past. If governors today cannot use the fiscal tools at their disposal to lower bankruptcy rates during gubernatorial campaigns, it seems unlikely that their predecessors with much weaker fiscal and regulatory tools at their disposal a century ago could have done so.Download: Download high-res image (299KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. Gubernatorial election campaigns typically spanned the two quarters before the quarter with an election.From the turn of the century through the end of the Roaring 20s, quarterly business bankruptcy rates for U.S. states exhibited stable patterns which are displayed in Table 1. Rates were low. Rates averaged 2.39 bankruptcies per thousand firms per quarter. At this time, failures of conglomerates operating in multiple states were rare except for railroads which are excluded from the data. So, business bankruptcy rates represent the ebbs and flows of economic activity in each state. Rates rose during recessions, peaking in 1908, 1915, and 1922. Rates fell during booms, particular the economic expansion of World War I. While rates varied over from year to year, averages over time varied less. About 2.24 firms per thousand failed from 1903 to 1916, the last year before the United States entered World War 1. About 2.7 per thousand failed during the 1920s. Rates varied across states. Interstate differences were stable over time. States with higher-than-average rates in the 1900s also had higher than average rates in the 1910s and 1920s.The data source is court filings of business bankruptcies. Two caveats concerning the data should be kept in mind. First, court filings understate failures of small firms. Creditors’ losses had to exceed $1000 dollars (which exceeded the median household income in 1900 and is equivalent in 2022 to $311,000 as a share of GNP) before suit could be filed in federal court. Legal fees could be substantial and exceed anticipated recoveries from insolvent small firms. In these cases, creditors could choose to pursue their rights with less expensive alternatives such as reporting the defaulter to credit bureaus, disseminating information about defaults in their local community, or cutting off the defaulter from future business deals. For these reasons, Dun's indicates court filings for small firm bankruptcies could represent three to five failures of small firms. Second, understatement increased during economic downturns. During contractions, asset values, firms’ net worth, and potential recoveries from liquidating insolvent businesses declined, while the clogging of court dockets slowed processing and increased costs. These changes reduced the expected benefits of pursuing debtors through courts, and according to Dun's, reduced the fraction of firms’ failures covered by court filings (Richardson and Gou, 2011). These caveats indicate that statistics on business bankruptcies illuminate the lower bound for firm failures and distress.
article.3. Campaigns and resolutions, 1903 to 20: While newspapers report many cases in which regulators delayed resolutions of banks at governors’ behest, most cases of regulatory discretion left little or no evidence in narrative sources. Their symptoms, however, can be observed in data on bank resolution rates. This section provides an initial examination of Progressive-Era data and compares the impact of gubernatorial elections on bank resolutions before and after the FDIC became the resolver for all insured commercial banks.The initial evidence appears in Fig. 4. Panels A and B plot average resolution rates for state-chartered banks in quarters preceding, containing, and following gubernatorial elections as well as a solid line indicating the average resolution rate in all other quarters. Gubernatorial campaigns in the Progressive Era typically spanned the two quarters before the election. Elections often occurred in the fall, typically early November. Campaigns began in the spring. During the two quarters preceding gubernatorial elections, resolution rates were lower than average in states where governors supervised bank regulation (Panel A) but not in states where judges oversaw bank resolutions (Panel B).Download: Download high-res image (327KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 4. Resolutions and Campaigns, Event Studies.Source: See Appendix for sources. Notes: All panels plot coefficients from event studies regressions. In Panels A and B, these values are calculated by regressing the resolution rate in each state in each quarter, Rit, on indicators for six time periods: (i) quarters containing gubernatorial elections, (ii) two quarters before quarters containing gubernatorial elections, (iii) one quarter before gubernatorial elections, (iv) one quarter after gubernatorial elections, (v) two quarters after gubernatorial elections, and (vi) all other quarters. Coefficients for (i) to (v) are indicated by dots. The horizontal line indicates the coefficient for indicator (vi). The coefficients report average resolution rates for each group of observations. In Panels C and D, these values are calculated by regressing the resolution rate in each state in each quarter, Rit, on indicators for each state, indicators for all but one time period, and indicators (i) to (v) above. Coefficients for indicators (i) to (v) are plotted. These coefficients indicate how average resolution rates in quarters before/during/after gubernatorial elections differed from the average resolution rate in all other quarters after extracting from each observation the average resolution rate for each state and each time period. Vertical lines indicate 90% confidence intervals. Panels A and C plot data from states where the governor oversaw the bank resolution process. Panels B and D plot data from states where the federal judiciary oversaw the bank resolution process.The difference could, of course, arise for many reasons. Over time, resolution rates and the number of states whose governors supervised bank resolutions rose. The frequency of gubernatorial elections declined. During the fourth quarter when most gubernatorial elections occurred, resolution rates peaked. Resolution rates differed across states, and those differences were correlated with the frequency of elections. Massachusetts, for example, had the most frequent gubernatorial elections (annual) and also one of the lowest average bank resolution rates. These factors could have generated the patterns apparent in the data.To alleviate concerns about these issues, Panels C and D indicate how resolution rates in quarters around elections differed from average after controlling for differences in resolution rates across states, over time, and through the seasons of the year. We calculate this value by regressing the resolution rate on indicators for each state, indicators for all but one time periods, and indicators for the quarter of and two quarters before and after gubernatorial elections. Regressions of this type are often termed an event study regression. The panels plot coefficients for the quarters around elections. This exercise yields clear results. In states whose governors oversaw bank resolution (C), the number of resolutions fell substantially during gubernatorial campaigns. In states where the federal judiciary oversaw bank resolutions (D), resolutions remained constant during gubernatorial campaigns.The decline during gubernatorial campaigns in states where governors supervised bank regulators was substantial and due in part to quarters when resolution rates fell to zero, or in other words, when regulators resolved no banks and instead deferred all resolutions until after the election. Evidence of this appears in Table 2. The table indicates that during campaigns in states whose governors oversaw bank resolutions the resolution rate fell by 0.7 banks per thousand per quarter in normal years and by 1.7 banks per thousand per quarter in years that experienced financial crises. These declines coincided with increases of about 10 percentage points in the fraction of states with resolution rates of zero (i.e., states where regulators resolved no banks in a quarter). In states where judges oversaw the resolution process, however, resolution rates during gubernatorial campaigns equaled or exceeded those during quarters without campaigns and the fraction of states reporting zero resolutions was lower (rather than higher, as in states whose governors oversaw bank resolution).Table 2. The mean resolution rate in those states outside of campaigns was 2.65 per thousand. On average, in other words, campaigns shifted the resolution rate from 2.65 per thousand to 0 in 10% of quarters with campaigns. The total decline in resolutions due to these shifts was 110 (= 0.1×868×480×(2.65/1000)). Third, gubernatorial campaigns typically spanned two quarters. No banks failed in 6 of 10 typical quarters (i.e., quarters with neither campaigns nor elections). We assume that regulators deferred bank resolutions in only one of the two quarters of a campaign.9 Combining these facts yields our back-of-the-envelope result.10 In one of three Progressive-Era gubernatorial campaigns where banks faced financial difficulties, regulators working for governors delayed the resolution of all troubled banks by about half a year to prevent bad news of bank failures from weakening their chances at the polls. In many more campaigns, regulators working for governors delayed the resolution of a bank or two for the same span of time. Overall, resolutions declined during gubernatorial campaigns by 458, with about a quarter of the decline concentrated in quarters where resolution rates fell to zero and three-quarters of the decline spread across other quarters.How did the impact of gubernatorial campaigns on bank resolutions during the Progressive Era, when regulators who resolved troubled banks worked directly for governors, differ from gubernatorial elections’ impact after the creation of the FDIC, when an independent federal agency served as the resolver for insured commercial banks? From 1934 to 2012, Liu and Ngo (2014) showed that 561 bank resolutions occurred in the pre-election window while 656 resolutions occurred in the post-election window. The difference of 95 was consistent with regulators delaying 48 (48≅95/2) resolutions from before until after gubernatorial elections. Forty-eight delayed resolutions over 79 years amounts to six-tenths of a delayed resolution per year (0.6 = 48/79) or approximately three delayed resolutions every five years.12 In that span, 1194 gubernatorial elections occurred, indicating the deferral of four-hundredths of a resolution per gubernatorial election (0.04≈48/1194) or 1 resolution every 25 gubernatorial elections. The overall gubernatorial campaign effect declined over time. From 1934–75, deferrals per election equaled 0.06; from 1976 to 2012, deferrals per election equaled 0.006. In contrast during the Progressive Era, deferrals per election, 0.842, was 140 times larger than modern times. Deferrals per year, 13.54, was 169 times larger.13In years without financial crises, it is important to note, the difference between the Progressive and modern eras was much smaller. From 1903–29, non-crisis deferrals during gubernatorial campaigns equaled 6.54 per year. From 1934–75, non-crisis deferrals equaled 1.06 per year. From 1976–2012, non-crisis deferrals equaled 1.6 per year. While these numbers were modest, bank resolution rates were low. Resolutions in non-crisis years averaged 68 from 1903 to 29, 12 from 1934 to 75 (when all years were non-crisis years), and 16 from 1976 to 2012. So, gubernatorial campaigns deferred resolutions of roughly 1 in 10 (≈ 6.54 of 68) troubled banks from 1903 to 1929, 1 in 12 troubled banks from 1934 to 1975 (≈ 1.06 of 12), and 1 in 10 from 1976 to 2012 (= 1.6 of 16). In non-crisis years, in other words, gubernatorial campaigns’ impact on resolutions fell after the introduction of the FDIC, but the primary cause of the decline was the nationwide decline in bank distress, which gave politicians fewer opportunities to intervene on banks’ behalf. Otherwise, the gubernatorial intervention rate (i.e., interventions relative to opportunities to intervene) during non-crisis years remained roughly constant over the twentieth century.That fact focusses attention on years of crisis. Most (7 out of 10) banks that failed since the foundation of the FDIC failed during the S&L Crisis of 1986 to 1992 and the Global Financial Crisis of 2007 to 2010, when bank failures rose to an average of 145 per year. Liu and Ngo (2014) concluded that gubernatorial campaigns did not influence bank resolutions during those events when, if anything, gubernatorial campaigns coincided with higher rather than lower resolution rates. In contrast, during crises in the Progressive Era, resolutions of state-chartered banks declined substantially during gubernatorial campaigns when governors oversaw bank resolution. As Table 3 shows, these declines occurred during the financial panics in 1907–8 and 1914, the depression in 1920–21, and the agricultural banking crisis of the 1920s. From 1922 to 1929, resolutions deferred during gubernatorial campaigns approached 1.5 per election or 26 per year, a rate so large that deferrals during four of these years exceeded the total of all deferrals during gubernatorial campaigns since the founding of the FDIC.
article.4. Campaigns and resolutions during the: Evidence presented in preceding sections indicates that resolution rates declined during gubernatorial campaigns in the Progressive Era when regulators subordinate to governors deferred resolutions in the run up to elections. This section corroborates the causal relationship. It demonstrates the correlation between campaigns and resolutions was not driven by seasonality, cyclicality, or shocks. The correlation existed only for banks under gubernatorial jurisdiction in states whose governors had authority over the resolution process. It did not exist for banks operating outside of governors’ jurisdiction, like nationally chartered banks, or in states whose governors lacked authority over resolutions. It did not exist for elections when governors were not on the ballot.We demonstrate these points by analyzing a panel of bank resolution and business bankruptcy rates aggregated by state and quarter for the contiguous 48 states excluding Wyoming, which is missing data for some observations. We digitize this information from an array of sources described in an appendix and link it with the extant corpus of economic and political data available at the same level of aggregation for the entire Progressive Era. Governors and their subordinates had the legal authority to delay state-bank resolutions and an incentive to do so. Governors did not manipulate these rates via policies which helped the broader economy, such as by increasing government spending, because those actions would have helped nationally chartered banks as well as state-chartered banks, since the two types of banks provided similar services and competed for customers.The last four columns of Table 6 ask whether our results held during normal times as well as years of crises. All specifications reveal resolutions declining during gubernatorial campaigns in states where governors oversaw resolutions. The fractional logit specification indicates that the total decline in resolutions due to oversight during campaigns during financial crises was nearly twice the decline during normal times. We present logit specifications in this table because they highlight large shifts towards zero during crises. We further explore this issue in an appendix which re-estimates Tables 5 and 6 with a zero-inflated beta mixture model that simultaneously estimates the probability that the resolution rate equaled zero and the relationship between the resolution rate and gubernatorial campaigns when the rate exceeded zero. This method shows the skewed distribution of resolution rates does not bias our results.Appendices and replication code further demonstrate the robustness of our results. A wide range of alternative methods and assumptions yield results similar to those presented in the body of the paper. These alternative specifications include (i) including up to four lags of all time-varying controls, (ii) limiting the right-hand side to the indisputably exogenous control, climate, while excluding all other time-varying controls, (iii) controlling for fiscal rather than calendar years as well as state-by-fiscal-year fixed effects, (iv) controlling for chronological shocks using date rather than year and quarter fixed effects, (v) clustering the error term by state rather than by date, and (vi) adding indicators for the third and/or fourth quarters before an election, to account for the possibility that regulators might shutter weak banks before the gubernatorial campaigning season got into full swing. The appendices also discuss selection and the date of the change from judicial to gubernatorial oversight. Our results do not change, however, when one restricts attention only to the 26 states that subjected resolutions to gubernatorial oversight before the Progressive Era. Results also do not change if we control for variations in resolutions that might have been induced by expectations of a changing policy regime or the timing of the transition to the new policy regime by including indicators for years leading up to the shift from judicial to regulatory supervision of resolutions. Overall, the results presented in the body of our paper and reinforced by our appendices reveal a strong relationship between gubernatorial campaigns and resolution rates in states with bank regulatory agencies under gubernatorial control during the Progressive Era.
article.5. Bank resolutions and business bankrup: The previous sections demonstrate bank resolutions declined during gubernatorial campaigns, often to zero, because governors and their subordinates sought to minimize bad news that could influence electoral outcomes. This result raises the question: did those interventions influence businesses that borrowed from banks?Reasons exist to expect the answer is yes. In the Progressive Era, loans to businesses were short term (Cohen et al., 2021; Del Angel, Gou, and Richardson, 2023). Many loans and all lines of credit were callable. Duration of term loans was typically less than 180 days, which was the maximum term for assets that could be discounted with the Federal Reserve. Commercial banks were the principal creditors of small trading and manufacturing firms and filed a substantial share of the lawsuits that triggered bankruptcies of those firms. In this environment, banks struggling to pay depositors typically called outstanding loans and refused to roll over maturing credit. Receivers appointed to liquidate failed banks as a matter of course called outstanding lines of credit and refused to renew term loans. This situation ensured that bank resolutions triggered contractionary cascades of commercial credit. This retrenchment could, in turn, trigger bankruptcies of businesses struggling to pay their debts. Deferring resolutions during gubernatorial campaigns could have delayed the onset of these contractionary credit cascades and subsequent surges in business bankruptcies. Whether they did so in a measurable amount is an empirical question.Measuring the relationship between bank resolutions and business bankruptcies is difficult because the phenomena are endogenous and interrelated. The issue which we are examining, however, provides an empirical lens on this issue. Gubernatorial campaigns timing was exogenous and predetermined. They can serve as an instrument to illuminate the relationship of interest. The basis for an effective IV is the relationship between the ultimate outcome of interest – in this case, business bankruptcy rates – and the instrument. The regression that establishes this relationship, which is presented in Table 7, is called the reduced form. Column 1 reports a baseline OLS estimate. The bankruptcies per one thousand businesses for each state i in quarter t is regressed on a constant and the instrument, Campaign*Agency. The instrument's coefficient indicates that during campaigns when the governor had the power to influence the bank resolution rate, the bankruptcy rate was 3 per 10,000 lower than the average rate of 24 per 10,000, a decline of 12.5% relative to the sample mean. Column 4 adds the full set of controls employed in Table 5. The signs and significance levels remain the same, although the magnitude declines to 2 per 10,000; this coefficient indicates that during campaigns in states whose governors oversaw resolutions, bankruptcies declined by about 8% relative to the sample mean.Table 7. Standard errors clustered by date in parentheses: *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.1.A key assumption underlying IV analysis is the exclusion criterion, which means that the instrument (in our case gubernatorial campaigns) influences the outcome of interest (in our case business bankruptcies) only via the channel under examination (gubernatorial machinations that delayed bank resolutions). To check the exclusion criterion, Columns 2 presents the reduced form for a sample which includes only observations for which the governor oversaw the resolution process (i.e., observations for which Agency = 1). In this case, the instrument is just Campaign, and the results resemble those for the full sample. Column 3 presents results for a sample including only observations where the governor did not oversee the resolution process (i.e. Agency = 0). In this case, the coefficient on Campaign is close to zero, and the hypothesis that it equals zero cannot be rejected. From these columns, we learn that when governors oversaw the resolution process business bankruptcies declined during gubernatorial campaigns. When governors did not oversee the resolution process, bankruptcies did not decline during gubernatorial campaigns. This observation substantiates the exclusion criteria, because bankruptcies should have declined during all gubernatorial campaigns, not only those when governors oversaw the bank resolution process, if governors used fiscal or regulatory tools to reduce bankruptcies in the run up to their election.A graphical presentation of this result appears in Fig. 5. It displays coefficients from event study regressions analogous to those in Fig. 4, which depicted the impact of gubernatorial campaigns on bank resolutions under different resolution regimes. Panels A and B plot average business bankruptcy rates in quarters preceding and following gubernatorial elections. Panels C and D plot average business bankruptcy rates in quarters preceding and following gubernatorial elections after extracting from each observation the average rate for its state and time-period. Controlling for time-period fixed effects reduces the post-election spike apparent in Figures A and B, which is due in large part to the seasonality of elections, which occur most often in the fourth quarter, and the seasonality of bankruptcies, which spike in the first quarter. Plots with and without controls clearly reveal declines in business bankruptcy rates during gubernatorial campaigns in states whose governors oversaw resolutions but not in states where the judiciary oversaw that task. Panels B and D plot data from states where the judiciary oversaw bank resolutions.A potential counterargument could be ‘perhaps governors gained fiscal and regulatory tools to influence business bankruptcies at the same time as they gained authority over bank resolutions.’ We checked the 21 states that adopted gubernatorially supervised resolution regimes during the decades that we study. We find no evidence of substantial changes in state budgets, budgetary procedures, or state regulatory policies other than those concerning commercial banks in the years that they revamped their bank regulatory regimes or the years immediately before or after. Moreover, as we noted in preceding sections, the U.S. constitution provided Congress with authority to set uniform rules for bankruptcies and regulate interstate commerce. Most legislation about these issues occurred in Washington, D.C. and impacted the entire nation except for decisions concerning bank regulation and resolution which Congress explicitly left to the states. Given these facts, we think this potential counterargument is unlikely to hold.The last four columns reinforce our conclusions concerning the exclusion criteria. The columns examine the relationship between bankruptcies and campaigns from 1980 to 2012. State governments’ role in the economy expanded substantially from the Progressive to the modern era. Expenditures by state governments as a share of GDP, for example, rose from less than 1% during 1900 to over 14% in 2012. If state-government fiscal actions lowered business bankruptcy rates during gubernatorial elections, one would expect the impact of those actions to be apparent today. Today, however, business bankruptcies rise on average during gubernatorial elections (see column 5), rather than fall as they did in the Progressive Era. The modern pattern appears is driven by the seasonality of gubernatorial elections, which now typically occur in November. After controlling for time and state fixed effects, gubernatorial campaigns are unrelated to business bankruptcy rates (column 6) as well as all bankruptcies (i.e., the sum of business and personal bankruptcies, see column 7 and 8). This finding remains true after controlling for state economic conditions such as changes in per capita personal income, unemployment, and prices. In sum, today, when governors’ fiscal and regulatory tools are much more powerful than those of their Progressive Era predecessors, governors are unable to reduce bankruptcy rates during gubernatorial campaigns. It seems unlikely that their predecessors influenced those rates with the much weaker tools at their disposal in the past.The literature, we believe, contains no explanation for the strong correlation between gubernatorial campaigns and business bankruptcies in the Progressive Era and the absence of this correlation before, when an independent judiciary oversaw bank resolutions, and after, when the FDIC oversaw resolutions of federally insured commercial banks. Our hypothesis explains this pattern. In the Progressive Era, when governors gained the ability to directly influence the timing of bank resolutions, their actions influenced credit available to firms and thus the timing of business bankruptcies. When governors’ ability to delay resolutions faded after the formation of the FDIC, the impact of their actions on firms also diminished.A straightforward approach to measuring the magnitude of the Progressive Era effect is to use an instrumental variable (IV) strategy. In typical applications with linear first and second stage regressions, the IV estimate would be the instrument's coefficient in the reduced form (measured in Table 7) divided by the instrument's coefficient in the first stage (which would be Column 8 in Table 5 but estimated by OLS). We apply this method to our discrete endogenous explanatory variable (EEV), an indicator for whether the resolution rate equals zero, Bit, since reductions in the resolution rate to zero were the clearest manifestations of gubernatorial interventions (additional specifications and robustness checks appear in appendices). All of the estimates are statistically significant and economically substantive. Column 3′s estimate is largest. Column 4′s estimate is slightly smaller and less precise, probably because the inclusion of additional control variables reduces the efficiency of the estimate. IV estimates range from −0.0013 to −0.0047.Table 8’s coefficients can be used to calculate the magnitude of the campaign-resolution-bankruptcy effect. Data from states whose governors oversaw resolutions indicate that typical gubernatorial intervention during campaigns shifted resolution rates from average to zero about half of the time when governors had an opportunity to do so, which amounted to a seventeen-percentage-point increase in the proportion of bank resolutions equal to zero during gubernatorial campaigns in states whose governors oversaw resolutions. This increase reduced average business bankruptcy rates during gubernatorial campaigns from 9.2% (0.092=−0.17×0.0013/0.0024) to 33.2% (0.3329=−0.17×0.0047/0.0024) relative to the sample mean. This effect may have been notable, but it only occurred during gubernatorial campaigns and was not a principal source of volatility for business failures for the overall economy.A concern for all IV regressions is the strength of the instrument. For our first stage estimates in Columns 2 and 3, the Kleibergen-Paap F-statistics and effective F-tests are above 10. Andrews et al. (2019) show that when the effective F-statistic is larger than 10, conventional t-tests perform well. So, we are confident that our estimates are not biased by weak instruments. The exception is the fourth column, where weak-instrument measures fall below conventional threshold, which means the weak-instrument issue could bias our estimates. For this reason, we report weak-instrument 95% confidence sets based on the linear combination method of Andrews (2018) as implemented by Liyang (2018). All the confidence sets exclude zero and lie in the negative range. This result corroborates our hypothesis that lowering the bank resolution rate to zero during gubernatorial campaigns in turn reduced the business bankruptcy rate.In our application, where we assume linearity in the first stage despite the distribution of the endogenous explanatory variable, the weak instrument issue may arise due to our assumption. Xu (2021) shows that in our case a consistent, stronger, and more efficient instrument is the fitted probabilities from a Probit estimated in the first stage, as suggested by Wooldridge (2010). Column 5 in Table 8 presents results from this two-step method when it is applied to our preferred specification from Column 3. Diagnostic statistics are far above thresholds for rejecting weak instruments. Coefficients on key variables remain statistically significant in the first and second stages, although the estimated impact of bank resolutions on business bankruptcies declines.For brevity, we do not report the estimates for states whose governors did not oversee bank resolutions. For these samples, the second stage is statistically insignificant. These regressions serve as a placebo test. Gubernatorial campaigns should not serve as an effective instrument in this setting, since bank resolutions did not systematically decline during campaigns in those states.A series of additional placebo tests (not reported) provide additional evidence consistent with our claims. The first placebos estimate the IV regressions on samples restricted to New England or Southern states.15 In New England, bank regulation was strict. Strictness stemmed both from Puritan government philosophies and the private regional check-clearing cooperative. Stringency kept resolution rates at zero in most (over 90%) quarters and near zero otherwise. Since banks seldom failed, governors seldom had the opportunity to lower bank resolution rates during campaigns. Given these circumstances, it is not surprising that restricting the sample to New England yields coefficients on our instrument close to zero. In Southern states, the Democratic party had dominated politics since Reconstruction. Democratic dominance reduced the incentive to delay resolutions preceding general elections since Democratic candidates almost always won. Given these circumstances, it is not surprising that restricting the sample to Southern states yields a coefficient on our instrument significantly lower than in states in the Mid-Atlantic, Midwest, Great Plains, Mountains, and Pacific West, where political competition during elections was often intense.The largest concern for our IV estimates arises from the nature of our data, particularly the dependent variable in the first stage. Bank resolution rates were not normally distributed. Instead, they were strongly skewed. Their median and mode were at the lower bound of zero. Appropriate estimators for this data, such as logit, fractional logit, and zero-inflated beta, are non-linear. In such circumstances, standard IV estimators could be biased, and standard diagnostic statistics could be misleading. Econometrics textbooks often refer to non-linear first stages as the ‘forbidden regression’ (Angrist and Pischke, 2009). Credible causal inference in this setting is an active area of research. In the body of this essay, we implement the solution recommended by Angrist and Pischke (2009 pp. 190–2), which is to estimate our IV with two-stage least squares (2SLS) using OLS in the first stage, as well as the two-step IV method of Wooldridge (2010) and Xu (2021). We also demonstrate the strength of the first stage and the reduced form, corroborate the exclusion criterion, and illuminate the mechanism linking the first and second stage and present evidence of it in operation. This statistical approach yields consistent estimates with transparent interpretations using standard methods that could be biased in finite samples.An alternative approach that should provide consistent estimates of causal effects in our setting is the control function method advocated by Wooldridge (2015). It is less likely to be biased because it can accommodate a non-linear first stage and allows us to bootstrap the standard errors of the coefficients and marginal effects in the second stage. We implement this method in an appendix. The method recovers signs, significance levels, and marginal effects similar to those recovered via standard methods. This finding indicates that in this application the bias due to the skewness of the first stage is probably small.Another way to address this issue is to compare the magnitude of the resolution-bankruptcy relationship recovered via our instrument to the magnitude estimated using instruments employed by other scholars. The most popular instruments are indicators for exogenous bank failures or panics identified using narrative or historical methods (Jalil, 2015). Estimates using our data and these alternative instruments, which we report in an appendix, yield estimates of bank resolutions’ impact on business bankruptcies that are similar in sign and significance but smaller in magnitude than our own. This result (like the results in our other appendices) strengthens our confidence in the accuracy of our estimates.
article.6. Discussion: Institutional choices have costs and benefits. Regulatory independence, which is a pillar of modern financial architecture, is no exception. Opponents of independence, like the plaintiffs in Seila Law vs. CFPB, argue that independent regulators’ aggressive actions reduce financiers’ profits, inhibit financial efficiency, and limit financial innovation. Direct political oversight might alleviate these harms. Our essay argues that regulatory independence plays an important role in the United States. It insulates financial institutions and their clients from short-term pressures inherent in electoral politics.Examining the magnitude of the gubernatorial-campaign effect in the Progressive Era and over the twentieth century reveals the value of regulatory independence. In states where governors oversaw the bank resolution process between 1903 and 1929, regulators deferred bank resolutions during gubernatorial campaigns at about 100 times the rate that their counterparts did from 1976 to 2012, when an independent federal agency, the FDIC, oversaw resolution of all federally insured banks. While the FDIC's insulation from politics today appears to be imperfect – since gubernatorial politics today influences bank resolution decisions at a low level in normal times, although not during the crises and recessions when most banks fail and when their failures may hinder industry and commerce – the FDIC's current insulation serves a purpose. Eliminating it would come at a cost. The timing of bank resolutions would shift substantially around gubernatorial elections.The mechanisms underlying the gubernatorial-campaign effect in the Progressive Era shed light on the mechanisms underlying the gubernatorial-campaign effect in modern times. In normal times, political pressures likely drive the result, since governors continue to supervise regulators of state-chartered banks and those regulators rotate examinations and share supervisory jurisdiction with the FDIC, although the federal agency has final say regarding resolutions of FDIC-insured institutions. So, governors today have less influence than they did in the past. The same mechanism appears to have been active during the S&L crisis years in the 1980s and early 1990s, when resolutions of state-chartered and state-insured banks surged after some gubernatorial elections. Rhode Island's banking crisis following its gubernatorial election in 1990 is an example (Qian et al., 2020). Liu and Ngo (2014) did not observe these events because they only analyzed FDIC-insured institutions.16 Our results for financial crises like 1907 and 2008 probably differ because governors oversaw regulators’ responses during the Progressive Era, while federal agencies directed the immediate and massive intervention to quell the financial panic following the failure of Lehman Brothers.Would shifting bank resolutions through time during gubernatorial campaigns impact the broader economy? Progressive Era evidence also sheds light on this question. Delaying bank resolutions during Progressive Era campaigns delayed bankruptcies of businesses. The magnitude of the business delay, however, was small compared to other sources of volatility in bankruptcy rates such as wars, recessions, and shifts in international economic conditions.What was the impact on banks themselves? Modern literature on this topic suggests that deferring resolutions, in some cases, increases the severity of loss. The ultimate example is the Savings & Loan Crisis, when losses born by the public expanded enormously when regulators deferred resolution of insolvent depositories in hopes that they could earn (or gamble) their way out of the grave (Kane, 1989; Sharma, 2022). Another example comes from the failure en masse of Rhode Island's state chartered and insured depositories immediately after the state's gubernatorial election in 1990. Preceding the election, the incumbent shifted funds to conceal fraud and losses in depositories under his jurisdiction as well as the insolvency of the state deposit insurance fund. After the election, revelation of the deceit triggered runs on state-insured depositories, forced the new governor to suspend operations at all state-insured institutions, and trapped 10% of the deposits in the state for about 6 months with some deposits not repaid for several years. These events reduced output and employment in the state for several years (Chen et. al., 2020). Progressive Era evidence on this issue, however, is limited. Newspaper accounts cited in Section 2 do indicate that political deferrals increased losses to depositors in some cases, but systematic statistical evidence on losses during resolutions of state-chartered banks in the Progressive Era is limited.Overall, our results indicate that subjecting regulators to political supervision could link electoral and economic cycles. While the aggregate impact of this linkage in the Progressive Era was large for banks and modest for businesses, the impact today could be larger. Over the last century, financial services’ share of GDP has risen to 9% from 2%. Use of credit by households and firms has also grown as has the financial leverage of most economic agents. The international influence of the American financial sector, the international prominence of the Federal Reserve, and the size of international capital flows in and out of the United States have also grown. Political shocks to the United States financial network may, therefore, have a larger impact on our nation and the world today than they did during the Gilded Age or Roaring 20 s.
